Article.ArticleTitle: Hypohydration is evident in elite orienteering athletes during a two-day race: a descriptive study.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study aimed to present changes in hydration status of orienteering athletes during a two-day race. Twenty elite male orienteers voluntarily participated in the study during a two-day race. Athletes' hydration status was measured two consecutive race days via urine specific gravity (USG) and fluid consumption was followed. There was a significant main effect of time on USG values (F2.32-20.89=3.70, p = 0.04, η2 = 0.29, ES = Large). USG values increased from the day one morning (1.019 ± 0.008) to post-race on day two (1.026 ± 0.003) (p = 0.04), from 3 h post-race on day one (1.018 ± 0.007) to post-race on day two (1.026 ± 0.003) (p = 0.01) and pre (1.019 ± 0.004) to post-race (1.026 ± 0.003) on day two (p = 0.001). Both races worsened hydration level of the athletes and they could not rehydrate post-race on day two despite fluid intake ad libitum. These findings suggest that orienteering athletes presented hypohydration following consecutive races as suggested by USG values on day two. Therefore, specific rehydration plan could be developed and applied for proper hydration status in these athletes. Clinical trial number: Clinical trial number is not applicable for this study.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Ethics approval and consent to participate: Ethical approval was received from Kastamonu University Non-Invasive Clinical Ethical Committee (2024-KAEK-74, date: 09/10/2024) and written informed consent form was received from each participant before the commencement of the study. Consent for publication: Not applicable. Competing interests: The authors declare no competing interests.
